title-group.article-title: Influencing factors for severe pertussis among Chinese children: A meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Immunization Program, Hangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Hangzhou Health Supervision Institution), Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China
bSchool of Public Health, Zhejiang Chinese Medical University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Yan Liu smileforever81@126.com Department of Immunization Program, Hangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Hangzhou Health Supervision Institution), Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310021, China.

fn.p: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: This study systematically evaluates factors influencing severe pertussis in Chinese children, providing scientific evidence for its prevention and treatment. A comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, CNKI, and Wanfang identified literature on factors influencing severe pertussis in Chinese children up to September 30, 2025. We conducted a meta-analysis, and data were combined using either fixed or random models. A total of 24 studies included 839 severe pertussis cases and 4,280 non-severe cases. Severe pertussis was significantly associated with several factors, with the top five strongest associations being: lung consolidation or atelectasis (OR: 15.44; 95%CI: 9.06–26.31), three concave sign (OR: 12.91; 95%CI: 5.46–30.53), elevated C-reactive protein (OR: 5.33; 95%CI: 3.00–9.49), unvaccinated with DTP vaccine (OR: 4.00; 95%CI: 2.86–5.58), and white blood cell count (WMD: 16.42 × 109/L; 95%CI: 9.49–23.36). Early identification of these factors and timely intervention, along with strengthening vaccination efforts for children, can help effectively reduce severe cases.

institution-wrap.institution: Zhejiang Science and Technology Plan for Disease Prevention and Control

funding-group.funding-statement: This study was supported by Zhejiang Science and Technology Plan for Disease Prevention and Control [Project No. 2025JK243].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Pertussis is an acute respiratory infectious disease caused by Bordetella pertussis that primarily affects infants and children under the age of five.1 The disease is characterized by long-term paroxysmal and spasmodic coughing, often accompanied by a “whoop” sound during inhalation.2 With the widespread use of diphtheria tetanus-pertussis (DTP) vaccine, the incidence of pertussis has shown a marked decline. However, since the 1980s, in countries with high vaccine coverage, such as Europe and the United States, have experienced a resurgence in cases after maintaining low incidence rates for many years, leading to outbreaks referred to as “resurgence of pertussis.”3 In China, through immunization efforts, the incidence of pertussis significantly decreased, with the annual average reported incidence rate falling to 0.2/100000 between 2006 and 2010.4 Nevertheless, recent years have also witnessed a resurgence of pertussis in China, with reported cases increasing. In 2024, there were 476,690 reported cases nationwide from January to December, approximately 11 times the 41,124 cases reported during the same period in 2023.5 In the year 2024, 476,690 cases of pertussis were reported nationwide, approximately 11 times the 41,124 cases reported during the same period in 2023. Notably, the number of deaths due to pertussis throughout 2024 was 31, which represents 1.55 times the total number of deaths from pertussis (20 cases) recorded over the 10-y period from 2014 to 2023.

Severe pertussis can lead to critical complications – including respiratory failure, whooping cough encephalopathy, and pulmonary hypertension – and is associated with high mortality.6 Therefore, early identification of severe pertussis is critical for improving patient outcomes. Definitions of severe pertussis vary across countries. In the United States, Berger et al. defined it as confirmed pertussis in children who either died or were admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU) for ≥24 hours.7 In Israel, Kuperman et al. diagnosed severe pertussis based on a positive PCR result for Bordetella pertussis and PICU admission.8 In contrast, in China, severe pertussis was diagnosed in children presenting with any of the following: recurrent apnea, hypoxemia, pertussis encephalopathy, or cardiovascular dysfunction.9 Given this heterogeneity in case definitions, restricting our meta-analysis to studies conducted in Chinese children helps ensure consistency in diagnostic criteria and comparability of findings across studies.

Existing studies indicate that severe pertussis in children is influenced by multiple factors, including age, vaccination status, white blood cell count, and cyanosis. However, findings regarding specific risk factors remain inconsistent across studies. For example, Zhao10 reported no significant association between age and severe pertussis, whereas Luo et al.11 found that age ≤3 months was significantly associated with severe pertussis (odds ratios, OR: 16.32; 95%CI: 2.36–112.76). Therefore, this study aims to conduct a meta-analysis to comprehensively evaluate the influencing factors for severe pertussis in Chinese children, with the goal of providing evidence-based recommendations to inform prevention and control strategies.

sec.title: Study registration
sec.p: This study was conducted in strict accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) reporting guidelines to ensure transparency and methodological rigor in the research process. The protocol of this study was registered in the Prospective Register of PROSPERO (Registration ID: CRD420251218767).

sec.title: Data searches
sec.p: The system retrieved Chinese journal databases such as China National Knowledge Infrastructure (CNKI), Wanfang Data Knowledge Service Platform, Chinese Medical Journal Full-text Database, and Chinese Biomedical Literature Database, as well as English databases such as Pubmed, Embase, and Web of Science, to collect literature on the related factors of severe pertussis in Chinese children. Chinese search terms: (severe) AND (children) AND (pertussis), English search terms: (severe) AND (children) AND (pertussis) AND (China OR Chinese). The search period was from the establishment of the database to September 30, 2025, supplemented by reference tracking and other methods. Diagnostic criteria for severe pertussis included children with pertussis experiencing any of the following: recurrent apnea; hypoxemia; pertussis encephalopathy; or cardiovascular dysfunction.9

sec.title: Literature inclusion and exclusion
sec.p: Inclusion Criteria: (1) Children under 18 y of age in China; (2) Clear diagnostic criteria for severe pertussis; (3) Observational study design, including cross-sectional, case-control, and cohort studies; (4) Control group with mild pertussis; (5) Ability to provide effect sizes and their 95% confidence intervals (CI) from analyses of factors influencing severe pertussis.
Exclusion Criteria: Literature that was published multiple times, case reports, reviews, and studies with insufficient data or unusable information were excluded.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Two investigators conducted an initial screening of the articles based on their titles and abstracts, and performed full-text searches on those deemed eligible. In cases of discrepancies, discussion and consultation with a third researcher were employed to reach a consensus. The extracted content included key information such as the first author, year of publication, study region, study duration, number of severe cases, number of non-severe cases, and relevant factors.

The study identified a total of 32 risk factors, including 6 demographic characteristics (sex, age ≤3 months, residence, prematurity, breast feeding, contact history), 15 clinical variables (spasmodic cough, vomiting after coughing, fever, paroxysmal cough, cyanosis, facial flushing at the end of paroxysm, wheezing, shortness of breath, lung rales, wheezing sound, conjunctival hemorrhage, three concave sign, hoarseness, unvaccinated with DTP vaccine, underlying diseases), 7 biological variables (white blood cell count, lymphocyte ratio, platelet count, increased CRP levels, alanine aminotransferase, liver function abnormalities, creatine kinase isoenzyme), and 4 comorbidities or complications (mixed infections, lung consolidation or atelectasis, pneumonia, emphysema).

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The quality of the eligible articles was independently evaluated by the same two reviewers. For cross‑sectional studies, the 11-item questionnaire recommended by the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ) was used.12 For each item, the answer “Yes” received 1 point, and the answers “No” or “Unclear” received 0 points, for a total possible score of 11 points. A higher total score indicated better quality of the literature. A score ≤3 was classified as low quality, 4–7 as moderate quality, and ≥8 as high quality. For case-control and cohort studies, the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS) was employed, with a maximum score of 9.13 Studies scoring 1–3 were classified as low quality, 4–6 as moderate quality, and 7–9 as high quality. If the quality was uncertain, the article was reviewed by a third reviewer.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analysis was performed using R version 3.5.2. For qualitative data, OR were used as the effect measure; for quantitative data, weighted mean differences (WMD) served as the effect measure. The heterogeneity among the included studies was assessed using the Q-test, combined with the I2 statistic to quantify the heterogeneity. If P < .10 and I2 ≥ 50.00%, this indicated significant heterogeneity among the studies, and a random-effects model was employed for the calculation of the pooled effect size. Conversely, P ≥ .10 and I2 < 50.00% indicated that there was no significant heterogeneity among the studies, and the fixed effect model is adopted. Sensitivity analysis was conducted by applying different effect models to the same data, comparing the results of the fixed-effects model and the random-effects model. If the results from both models were consistent, this indicated a certain degree of reliability in the pooled results; if the results differed, it suggested that the pooled outcome may be unreliable. Leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was conducted to examine whether single studies had a disproportionately excessive influence.14 Publication bias was assessed using Egger’s test combined with funnel plots for studies with 10 or more publications; if P > .05, this indicated a low likelihood of publication bias. The significance level was set at α = 0.05.

sec.title: Search results
sec.p:
According to the search strategy, a total of 506 publications were identified. After rigorously applying the inclusion and exclusion criteria, 24 relevant studies were ultimately included. A flowchart of the search and selection process is presented in Figure 1. Figure 1.
Flow diagram for selection of studies for inclusion in this meta-analysis.Flowchart of study selection process for meta-analysis.

sec.title: Descriptions of the included studies
sec.p:
Of the 24 studies, 21 studies were written in Chinese and 3 studies written in English. A total of 5,119 participants were involved in the studies, including 839 cases of severe pertussis and 4,280 cases of non-severe pertussis. All participants were enrolled from hospital-based settings. These studies were conducted in 14 different provinces and cities from 2011 to 2024. Geographically, 12 studies were conducted in southern China, and 10 studies were conducted in northern China. Among the 24 included studies, 23 employed a cross-sectional design and one was a case-control study; no cohort studies were included. The quality scores of the included studies ranged from 7 to 9 points, with an average score of 7.96 ± 0.16 points. The characteristics of the 24 included studies are shown in Table 1.Table 1.
Description of included studies in the meta-analysis.
Author/Publication yearLanguageProvinceThe year of studyAgeaMild group (n)Severe group (n)Study designQuality

sec.title: Results of meta-analysis
sec.p:
Severe pertussis risk factors were classified into demographic characteristics, clinical variables, biological variables and comorbidities or complications. The heterogeneity test results showed that among the 32 influencing factors, male sex, contact history, paroxysmal cough, lung rales, conjunctival hemorrhage, three concave sign, hoarseness, underlying diseases, platelet count, increased C-reactive protein (CRP) levels, alanine aminotransferase, creatine kinase isoenzyme, pneumonia, and emphysema exhibited no statistically significant heterogeneity between the included studies (I2 < 50.00% and P ≥ .10); therefore, the fixed-effect model was used to pool the relevant effect sizes. For the remaining 18 factors, heterogeneity tests indicated I2 ≥ 50.00% and P < .10, suggesting some degree of heterogeneity, and the random-effects model was employed to pool the results, as shown in Table 2.Table 2.
Meta-analysis of factors influencing severe pertussis in Chinese children.
VariablesNumbers of studiesOR/WMD (95%CI)ZPHeterogeneityModel
I2 (%)P
Demographic Characteristics
Male220.98(0.83–1.15)−0.283.77715.6.253Fixed

Lung consolidation or atelectasis1415.44(9.06–26.31)7.629<.00177.8<.001Random

Emphysema32.47(1.36–4.47)2.985.0030.0.524Fixed
aFor quantitative data, WMD served as the effect measure.

sec.title: Demographic characteristics
sec.p: Demographic characteristics associated with severe pertussis included age ≤3 months (OR: 2.87; 95%CI: 1.65–4.99; P < .001) (10/24 studies), prematurity (OR: 3.20; 95%CI: 1.03–9.93; P = .044) (8/24 studies) and contact history (OR: 1.78; 95%CI: 1.42–2.22; P < .001) (13/24 studies). In contrast, male (OR: 0.98; 95%CI: 0.83–1.15; P = .777) (22/24 studies), rural (OR: 1.75; 95%CI: 0.81–3.76; P = .151) (4/24 studies) and breast feeding (OR: 0.85; 95%CI: 0.38–1.90; P = .685) (3/24 studies) were not associated with severe pertussis.

sec.title: Clinical variables
sec.p: Clinical variables associated with severe pertussis included fever (OR: 2.81; 95%CI: 2.02–3.91; P < .001) (23/24 studies), cyanosis (OR: 3.84; 95%CI: 2.01–7.34; P < .001) (13/24 studies), lung rales (OR: 3 .50; 95%CI: 2.70–4.55; P < .001) (15/24 studies), three concave sign (OR: 12.91; 95%CI: 5.46–30.53; P < .001) (4/24 studies), unvaccinated with DTP vaccine (OR: 4.00; 95%CI: 2.86–5.58; P < .001) (18/24 studies) and presence of underlying diseases (OR: 1.88; 95%CI: 1.17–3.02; P = .009) (5/24 studies). In contrast, spasmodic cough (OR: 1.11; 95%CI: 0.69–1.78; P = .675) (12/24 studies), vomiting after coughing (OR: 1.12; 95%CI: 0.75–1.67; P = .571) (12/24 studies), paroxysmal cough (OR: 0.81; 95%CI: 0.65–1.01; P = .058) (17/24 studies), facial flushing at the end of paroxysm (OR: 1.00; 95%CI: 0.51–1.96; P = .994) (7/24 studies), wheezing (OR: 1.57; 95%CI: 0.92–2.67; P = .099) (7/24 studies), shortness of breath (OR: 3.02; 95%CI: 0.87–10.50; P = .082) (4/24 studies), wheezing sound (OR: 2.60; 95%CI: 0.19–35.62; P = .750) (2/24 studies), conjunctival hemorrhage (OR: 0.49; 95%CI: 0.06–4.05; P = .509) (2/24 studies) and hoarseness (OR: 0.85; 95%CI: 0.23–3.10; P = .809) (2/24 studies) were not associated with severe pertussis.

sec.title: Biological variables
sec.p: Biological variables associated with severe pertussis included increased CRP levels (OR: 5.33; 95%CI: 3.00–9.49; P < .001) (3/24 studies) and higher white blood cell count (WMD: 16.42 × 109/L; 95%CI: 9.49–23.36; P < .001) (13/24 studies). In contrast, lymphocyte ratio (WMD:-4.12; 95%CI: ‒13.08–4.83; P = .366) (4/24 studies), platelet (WMD: ‒3.99 × 109/L; 95%CI: ‒25.52–17.55; P = .717) (5/24 studies), alanine aminotransferase (WMD: 0.61 U/L; 95%CI: ‒3.78–4.70; P = .769) (3/24 studies), liver function abnormalities (OR: 1.68; 95%CI: 0.78–3.59; P = .183) (5/24 studies) and creatine kinase isoenzyme (WMD:-0.13 U/L; 95%CI:‒1.83–1.57; P = .881) (2/24 studies) were not associated with severe pertussis.

sec.title: Comorbidities or complications
sec.p: Comorbidities or complications associated with severe pertussis included mixed infections (OR: 2.98; 95%CI:2.16–4.11; P < .001) (17/24 studies), lung consolidation or atelectasis (OR: 15.44; 95%CI: 9.06–26.31; P < .001) (14/24 studies), pneumonia (OR: 3.34; 95%CI: 2.49–4.49; P < .001) (11/24 studies) and emphysema (OR: 2.47; 95%CI: 1.36–4.47; P = .003) (3/24 studies).

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p:
For factors reported in 10 or more studies, Egger’s test was utilized to assess publication bias. Publication bias assessment suggested that there might be publication bias for factors such as vomiting after coughing, white blood cell count, mixed infections, and pneumonia (P < .05) (Table 3, Figures S1–S4). For vomiting after coughing, Egger’s test showed a significant negative intercept (intercept: –3.09; 95%CI: –5.06–1.12; P = .006). In contrast, positive intercepts were observed for white blood cell count (intercept:8.43; 95%CI: 0.95–15.91; P = .001), mixed infections (intercept: 3.15; 95%CI: 0.16–6.14; P = .040), and pneumonia (intercept:1.63; 95%CI: 0.61–2.66; P = .006).Table 3.
Egger’s test results for studies with 10 or more publications.
VariablestP
Demographic Characteristics

Age ≤ 3 months0.07.942
Contact history0.41.691

Vomiting after coughing−3.50.006

Biological variables

Comorbidities/Complications
Mixed infections2.24.040
Lung consolidation or atelectasis1.32.213
Pneumonia3.60.006

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p:
For studies examining the same influencing factors (≥2 studies), sensitivity analyses were conducted using both fixed-effect and random-effect models, with corresponding OR and 95%CI calculated. The results indicated that, in this study, with the exception of rural, underlying diseases, wheezing, shortness of breath, wheezing sound, and liver function abnormalities, the other results did not show significant differences or exhibited only small differences. This suggests that the meta-analysis results for most influencing factors in this study are relatively stable (Table 4).Table 4.
Sensitivity analysis of the studies in different effect models.
VariablesFixed-effects modelRandom-effects model
OR/WMD95%CIOR/WMD95%CI
Demographic Characteristics

Age ≤ 3 months2.992.26–3.962.871.65–4.99

Contact history1.781.42–2.221.781.34–2.37

emphysema2.471.36–4.472.581.42–4.69
aFor quantitative data, WMD served as the effect measure.

The leave-one-out sensitivity analysis revealed that the study by Wang et al.16 had a substantial impact on the pooled effect for rural residence; after excluding this study, rural residence was associated with an increased risk of severe pertussis (OR: 2.59, 95%CI: 1.65–4.06). The study by Wang et al.16 markedly influenced the result for breastfeeding; upon its removal, breastfeeding was found to reduce the risk of severe pertussis (OR: 0.56, 95%CI: 0.36–0.89). The study by Li et al.25 (OR: 1.39, 95%CI: 0.80–2.40) had a considerable impact on the association for underlying diseases; after excluding this study, underlying diseases were no longer significantly associated with severe pertussis. The study by Wang17 substantially affected the result for spasmodic cough; its exclusion resulted in an increased risk of severe pertussis associated with spasmodic cough (OR: 1.37, 95%CI: 1.02–1.84). The study by Kang et al.23 had a notable influence on the finding for paroxysmal cough; after its removal, paroxysmal cough was associated with a decreased risk of severe pertussis (OR: 0.76, 95%CI: 0.60–0.96). The study by Wang et al.19 (OR: 12.27, 95%CI: 0.69–219.03) exerted a strong influence on the effect of three concave sign; after excluding this study, three concave sign showed no significant association with severe pertussis. For the remaining factors, the results did not change significantly after the sequential exclusion of individual studies, indicating that the findings were relatively robust (Table S1).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In recent years, many countries with high pertussis vaccination coverage, including China, have experienced the “resurgence of pertussis,” posing a significant challenge to public health. This has caused widespread concern both domestically and internationally. Pertussis cases surged, and deaths hit a record high in China in 2024. Severe pertussis poses a serious threat to life and health, as it carries a risk of death due to the severe complications that commonly accompany the disease. Most studies on severe pertussis at home and abroad are mainly focus on case reports,37 which are limited to describing clinical characteristics. There is relatively little research on the factors influencing the incidence of severe pertussis. Early identification of high-risk patients can help healthcare professionals implement timely and targeted interventions to improve patient prognosis, reduce the incidence of complications and case fatality rates, enhance the quality and efficiency of healthcare, and lessen the economic burden on families as well as the consumption of public health resources caused by severe pertussis. This study aims to clarify the potential influencing factors for severe pertussis through a meta-analysis of relevant literature.

This study found that being ≤3 months of age is a risk factor for severe pertussis, which is consistent with the findings of Straney et al.38 Infants under three months of age have immature respiratory and cardiovascular regulation, making them susceptible to complications such as pulmonary hypertension, cardiopulmonary failure, and neurological damage. Due to the atypical presentation of symptoms in infants, primarily manifesting as respiratory pauses, cyanosis, and dyspnea, misdiagnosis and missed diagnoses are common, which can lead to severe pertussis or even death.19 Premature infants, due to their immature immune systems and underdeveloped thoracic structures, experience narrower airways and more severe disturbances of ciliary movement and excessive mucus secretion caused by pertussis toxins, making them more susceptible to severe pertussis.39 Additionally, this study identified that children with a history of exposure to individuals with persistent cough are more prone to severe pertussis, which may be linked to the adult-infant transmission pattern.40

Pertussis-containing vaccine is the most effective measure for preventing and reducing severe pertussis cases and mortality in infants. This meta-analysis indicates that lack of DTP vaccination is a risk factor for severe pertussis in children. The World Health Organization recommends that all countries complete DTP vaccination as early as possible, with the first dose administered no later than 8 weeks of age (2 months).41 In recent years, although the reported coverage rate of the 3-dose DTP vaccine in China has remained above 99%, the reported incidence rate of pertussis has shown an upward trend.42 Due to the increased risk of pertussis among younger infants and school-aged children in our country, as of January 1, 2025, the immunization schedule will be adjusted from the previous protocol of administering one dose of Diphtheria-Tetanus-Acellular Pertussis vaccine (DTaP) at 3, 4, 5, and 18 months, with a booster dose of the diphtheria and tetanus combined vaccine at 6 y, to a new schedule of administering one dose of DTaP at 2, 4, 6, and 18 months, and at 6 y.43 The increased susceptibility of young infants to severe pertussis may be related to delayed vaccination – under the previous schedule, the first dose was not administered until 3 months of age, leaving young infants without vaccine-induced specific immune protection during their earliest months of life. The WHO recommends that pregnant women receive one dose of tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis vaccine (Tdap) during the second or third trimester.44 Some countries have already integrated maternal vaccination into their routine national immunization programs, whereas China has not yet done so. Therefore, further exploration is needed on how to introduce booster vaccination for pregnant women based on the current immunization schedule, in order to enhance population immunity and control the transmission of Bordetella pertussis.

The meta-analysis results indicate that clinical manifestations such as cyanosis, and three concave sign were significantly more prevalent in the severe group compared to the ordinary group, which is consistent with findings from international studies.45 Due to the narrow airways and underdeveloped immune systems in children, Bordetella pertussis can proliferate extensively following infection, leading to airway obstruction and atypical manifestations such as cyanosis, which can easily progress to severe cases.46 In contrast, clinical manifestations such as spasmodic cough, vomiting after coughing, and paroxysmal cough showed no significant difference with severe pertussis. Therefore, it is essential to promptly recognize symptoms and signs such as cyanosis and three concave sign during clinical diagnosis and treatment, and to implement early intervention measures to delay or mitigate the deterioration of the condition.

This study found that elevated white blood cell count is a risk factor for severe pertussis, consistent with several domestic and international research findings.47–49 The toxin produced by Bordetella pertussis – pertussis toxin – can promote the proliferation of leukocytes and lymphocytes, causing the release of lymphocytes from the peripheral blood reserve pool into the circulating pool, which results in an abnormal increase in white blood cell counts. However, due to the poor deformability of leukocytes, they are prone to forming aggregates that can cause embolism in the narrow pulmonary capillary beds, leading to hypoxemia and pulmonary hypertension, which can progress to severe pertussis.50 Therefore, if a pertussis patient exhibits a sustained increase in white blood cell count, it is crucial to pay close attention to the potential for progression to severe disease. CRP is an acute-phase protein synthesized by the liver, which typically increases during acute inflammation and is commonly used clinically as a marker for bacterial infections. This suggests that if children with pertussis exhibit elevated CRP levels, there should be heightened vigilance regarding the potential progression to severe pertussis.

Furthermore, this study indicate that children with mixed infections tend to exhibit more severe clinical symptoms and a higher incidence of complications compared to those with isolated pertussis infections.38 The mechanisms underlying the occurrence of mixed infections may involve multiple factors: the overlapping epidemiological periods of certain pathogens51; the types of locally prevalent pathogens, etiological detection methods, and clinical submission situations, etc., may also have an impact on it; and pertussis toxin may suppress the immune function of the host, thereby increasing susceptibility to other pathogens.52 The mixed infections primarily involved co-infection with respiratory pathogens such as respiratory syncytial virus, influenza virus, parainfluenza virus, adenovirus, haemophilus influenzae, and streptococcus pneumoniae. These pathogens often present with cough as a prominent clinical symptom, which may overlap with the clinical manifestations of pertussis. If pertussis testing is not conducted concurrently, this can easily lead to missed or misdiagnoses. Therefore, when a child exhibits symptoms of severe pertussis, in addition to administering targeted anti-infection treatment against Bordetella pertussis and providing symptomatic supportive care, it is also essential to actively screen for possible mixed infections to ensure comprehensive and effective treatment. Fever is typically a clinical manifestation resulting from the interaction of Bordetella pertussis with other pathogens. Therefore, clinicians should closely monitor whether children with pertussis exhibit fever and remain vigilant for potential bacterial or viral infections to prevent the deterioration of their condition.

Our study had a few limitations. First, 23 of the included studies had a cross-sectional design and one was a case-control study. Due to these design constraints, the results can only reveal potential causal clues and associations, and can’t establish definitive causal relationships. While some variables (e.g., lung consolidation, three concave sign, increased CRP levels, and white blood cell count) were statistically associated with severe pertussis in our study, their role as true predictive risk factors warrants further investigation through prospective cohort studies with clear temporal definitions. Second, several factors, including paroxysmal cough, white blood cell count, mixed infections, and pneumonia, showed evidence of potential publication bias. This may be attributed to inherent clinical and methodological heterogeneity across primary studies (e.g., variations in the definition of ‘paroxysmal cough’ or differences in diagnostic techniques for ‘mixed infections’). Third, the variation in participant age across studies–ranging from infants under one year to children up to 18 y–introduces potential heterogeneity. Given that risk factors likely differ by age (e.g., incomplete vaccination in infants), the lack of standardized age groupings precluded meaningful subgroup analysis to explore this variation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: By identifying the relevant influencing factors associated with severe pertussis in children and implementing corresponding intervention measures, early detection, diagnosis, and treatment of the disease can be achieved, effectively delaying disease progression, reducing severity, shortening the duration of illness, and optimizing the prognosis for affected children to a certain extent. These findings highlight the urgent need for great efforts to strengthen pertussis epidemiological and laboratory surveillance, conduct active surveillance, and enhance the sensitivity of the surveillance system. In the future, more high-quality epidemiological studies on factors influencing severe pertussis are required.

bio.p: Yan Liu, Director, Department of Immunization Program, Hangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Hangzhou Health Supervision Institution), Chief Physician, Master of Public Health, Zhejiang University, Master Supervisor Tutor, Chinese Field Epidemiology Training Program (CFETP), Member, National Immunization Program Technical Working Group (Pneumococcal Vaccine Working Group), Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention (China CDC).

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The dataset used in the study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

sec.title: Academic contributions
sec.p: She has published many academic papers related to pertussis, including ‘Economic burden of pertussis in children: A single-center analysis in Hangzhou, China.’ She has presided over a number of provincial and municipal science and technology projects.

sec.title: Main research focus
sec.p: Prevention and control of vaccine-preventable diseases.

sec.title: Reviewer disclosures
sec.p: Peer reviewers on this manuscript have no relevant financial or other relationships to disclose.

sec.title: Supplemental material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2026.2648369
